prompt:
Security review killed our itertools dependency (supply-chain policy: zero external crates in the agent binary), and it turns out we only ever used three adapters. So we're writing them ourselves, properly — as real Iterator structs, not collect-into-a-Vec fakes. I've already written the acceptance tests (tests/adapters.rs) including probe-based laziness tests that count every next() call on the source; they are the contract. Create src/lib.rs in this crate (rs-iteradapt). Tests, Cargo.toml, .gitignore stay untouched; stdlib only.

Public API:
- pub trait IterAdapters: Iterator + Sized, blanket-implemented for every Iterator (impl<I: Iterator> IterAdapters for I), with three provided methods:
  - fn chunk_by<F>(self, same_group: F) -> ChunkBy<Self, F> where F: FnMut(&Self::Item, &Self::Item) -> bool — yields Vec<Self::Item> groups of consecutive elements. The predicate is called with (&previous_element, &candidate) — the immediately preceding element, like slice::chunk_by — and the candidate joins the group while it returns true.
  - fn dedup_by_key<K, F>(self, key: F) -> DedupByKey<Self, K, F> where K: PartialEq, F: FnMut(&Self::Item) -> K — yields elements, skipping any whose key equals the key of the last YIELDED element (consecutive runs collapse to their first element; non-adjacent repeats survive).
  - fn intersperse(self, separator: Self::Item) -> Intersperse<Self> where Self::Item: Clone — yields the separator between adjacent elements; never leading or trailing.
- The three structs public with exactly those names and generic parameter orders: ChunkBy<I, F>, DedupByKey<I, K, F>, Intersperse<I> (the tests name these types explicitly).

The part I actually care about — the laziness/pull contract, which the probes pin exactly:
1. Constructing an adapter pulls NOTHING from the source (no eager peek in the constructor).
2. Bounded lookahead, always buffered: chunk_by pulls through the first element of the NEXT group to find the boundary and must hand that element back in the following group without re-pulling; intersperse may look one element ahead to decide whether a separator is due, and that element must come back out of the buffer.
3. Once the source has returned None you must NEVER call next() on it again (sources are not guaranteed to be fused). The probes assert the pull counter freezes after exhaustion.
4. intersperse clones the separator exactly once per emitted separator and never clones items; chunk_by and dedup_by_key must not require Clone on items at all (the tests push non-Clone types through them).
5. dedup_by_key stores the last yielded KEY (K), not the element, so items pass through by move.

Expected pull schedules, so there's no ambiguity (source shown as a list): chunk_by over [1,1,2] with equality: construction 0 pulls; first group [1,1] costs 3 pulls; second group [2] costs 1 more (the buffered 2 plus the None); subsequent next() calls cost 0. dedup over [1,1,1,2]: 1 pull to yield 1, then 3 to yield 2, then 1 to see the end. intersperse over [10,20]: 1 pull → 10, 1 pull → separator (successor buffered), 0 pulls → 20, 1 pull → None. Acceptance: cargo test --offline is green.
